Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}} |Foundation = (legendary) 11th {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|qid=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}} |Main shrine={{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternative name = Ninomiya Daimyojin/Ninomiya Myojin Shrine |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival=2nd Sunday of October|Shinto ritual = Otsutsugayu Festival ({{ill|lt=January 15th|Q2260|qid=Q2260|1=January 15}})
{{ill|lt=Kokufu Festival|Q11420919|qid=Q11420919|1=Kokufu-sai}} ({{ill|lt=May 5th|Q2550|qid=Q2550|1=May 5}}) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|lat_deg=35|lat_min=18|lat_sec=0.6|lon_deg=139|lon_min=14|lon_sec=17.1|label=Kawagu Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|18|0.6|N|139|14|17.1|E}}}} }} [[File:Kawawa-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]]'''Kawawa Shrine'' is located in {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|qid=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nakagun|Q1202849|qid=Q1202849|1=Naka District, Kanagawa}}{{ill|lt=Ninomiya Town|Q1354853|qid=Q1354853|1=Ninomiya, Kanagawa}} in Yamanishi. {{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} It is written in "{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}" in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}). It is said to be one of the 13 Shikinai shrines in Engi Province (small shrines) in {{ill|lt=Sagami Province|Q1053732|qid=Q1053732|1=Sagami Province}}. {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}}.A traditional {{ill|lt=festival|Q1322|qid=Q1322|lt=41|1=José Manuel Balmaceda}}, {{ill|lt=Kokufu Festival|Q11420919|qid=Q11420919|lt=Kounomachi|1=Kokufu-sai}} is one of the five Sagami shrines that participates in {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya #Ninomiya, Sannomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}. The main ritual of the festival, ``Zaikan'', is a combination of {{ill|lt=Aibu Kunizo|Q11582190|qid=Q11582190|lt=Aibu|1=sagamu no kuni no miyatsuko}} (Sagamu) and {{ill|lt=Shicho Kunizo|Q11481128|qid=Q11481128|lt=Isonaga|1=Isonaga}} (Shinaga, also written as ``Shicho''), which is the name of Sagami Province. This is said to have been caused by {{ill|lt=history|Q1210892|qid=Q1210892|1=Chinese proverbs}}, who fought over whether {{ill|lt=Samukawa Shrine|Q48744|qid=Q48744|1=Samukawa Shrine}} or our company should be designated as Sagami Province {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}}. The name of the location, ``Ninomiya Town,'' is a nod to our company, and has also been referred to as ``''Ninomiya Daimyojin'''' or ``''Ninomiya Myojin Shrine'''' since ancient times. === Enshrined God === ``Kawagata Shrine Magazine''``Kawagata Shrine Magazine'' is a bookmark for worship distributed by our company. According to , the following five deities are currently enshrined. * {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|qid=Q276944|lt=Daimyo Takamikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}} (Onamuji no Mikoto) - The god who cultivated the land of Japan. * {{jalink|Oimonoimino no Mikoto|Q3270544|Oimonoimino Mikoto} - A god who was instrumental in promoting industry. * {{ill|lt=Shinatsuhiko|Q1073925|qid=Q1073925|lt=Class Nagatsuhiko no Mikoto|1=Shinatsuhiko}} (Shinatsuhiko no Mikoto) - God of wind and breath. * Class Nagatsuhime no Mikoto (Shinatsuhime no Mikoto) - Same as above. A couple deity who pioneered the country of Isonaga. * {{ill|lt=Kotori Hime|Q11626419|qid=Q11626419|lt=Kutori Hime Mikoto|1=Princess Sotoorihime}} (Sotohorihime no Mikoto) - A miraculous god who protects safe childbirth."Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Places - 11 Kanto" Kenichi Tanikawa (ed.) From "Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Places - 11 Kanto" Hakusuisha, December 1984. According to , "{{ill|lt=New Edition of Sagami Province Fudoki|Q11503942|qid=Q11503942|1=Shinpen Sagami no Kuni Fudoki-kō}} Volume 40" compiled in {{ill|lt=Tenpo|Q1369045|qid=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}}12 ({{ill|lt=1841|Q7622|qid=Q7622|1=1841}}) Edited by Ito Ashida From "Dainippon Topographical Series 15, New Sagami Province Fudoki Draft Volume 2", Yuzankaku, September 1962. In , there are three deities enshrined: Itsuhime no Mikoto, Omonoki no Mikoto, and Kura Nagatsuhiko no Mikoto, but in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasunaga|1=UNKNOWN}} 5th year ({{ill|lt=1776|Q7703|qid=Q7703|1=1776}}), {{jalink|chief priest|Q1145} 3500}} From the description in the ``Osai-no-Shiki Tradition'', which was transcribed by Mr. Futami, it can be confirmed that {{ill|lt=Hachimanjin|Q261637|qid=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} was enshrined in the middle of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}. Regarding this, "Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites - 11 Kanto" "Ninomiya Kawago Shrine Engi-sho" was compiled by {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|qid=Q15888735|1=Kannushi (disambiguation)}} Shintaro Futami and is owned by Kawago Shrine. It is also included in ``Shinto Taikei Shrines Edition 16 Suruga, Izu, Kai, and Sagami Province'' (March 1980), edited by the Shinto Taikei Compilation Association. According to , during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|qid=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, {{ill|lt=Taaya County|Q17177016|qid=Q17177016|lt=Yoaya|1=Yurugi district}} and {{jalink|Ashigarashimo County|Q1193172|Ashi It is said that when the east and west coasts of both districts were Isonaga Province, Ayaha Miyatsuko, the ruler of Isonaga Province {{ill|lt=kokuzō|Q2483673|qid=Q2483673|lt=kokuji|1=Kuni no miyatsuko}}, founded it to protect the country in accordance with an imperial order. ``Shikinaisha Investigation Report Volume 11'' also states that this area was the center of Isonagakuni, and that our shrine was connected to {{ill|lt=Shinagakunizo|Q11481128|qid=Q11481128|lt=Isonagakunizo|1=Isonagakunizo}} (Shinaga no Kuni no Miyako). According to "Shinhen Sagami Province Fudoki Kou Volume 40" , the place name ``Kawago'' is said to come from the {{ill|lt=Nakamura River (Ashigarakami District)|Q11365555|qid=Q11365555|lt=Oshikiri River|1=Nakamura River}} flowing in this area in ancient times, and the name of Kawago Shrine is also derived from the place name.Furthermore, “Ninomiyagawa Kojinsha Lucky Book”The reason why she is considered the wife of Emperor Konkyo is based on the description in ``{{ill|lt=Nihonshoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}} Volume 13.'' It is said that they prayed for the safe birth of the prince, but ``Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites - 11 Kanto'' says that all of this is nothing more than folklore. In {{ill|lt=Extension (era name)|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Extension|1=Enchō}} in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}), it was elevated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi-sha|1=Shikinaisha}} and small shrine according to the ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}''. It is said that {{ill|lt=MINAMOTO no Yoshiie|Q32613|qid=Q32613|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} made offerings during the {{ill|lt=Early Nine Years' War|Q1187448|qid=Q1187448|1=Former Nine Years' War}} and {{ill|lt=Gōsannen's War|Q1187411|qid=Q1187411|1=Gosannen War}}."{{ill|lt=Azuma Kagami|Q1042324|qid=Q1042324|1=Azuma Kagami}}" {{ill|lt=Takehisa|Q1193933|qid=Q1193933|1=Kenkyū}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1192|Q19739|qid=Q19739|1=1192}}) Article dated August 9th It is said that Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} prayed to ``Ninomiya Kawano Daimyojin'' for the safe delivery of {{ill|lt=Masako Hojo|Q463961|qid=Q463961|1=Hōjō Masako}}, and {{ill|lt=Shinba|Q11591047|qid=Q11591047|1=Shinme}} was dedicated to it. In addition, Minamoto no Yoritomo constructed a shrine building and donated some shrine territory during the Kenkyu era. Our company was called Ninomiya {{ill|lt=Myojin|Q5369590|qid=Q5369590|lt=Daimyojin|1=Myōjin}}, Ninomiya Myojin Shrine, but from ``Basic Research on the Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries'' edited by the Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries Research Group, Iwata Shoin, February 2000. states that this ``Azuma Kagami'' is the first document that refers to our company as Ninomiya. However, from ``Ichinomiya Nooto Nooto Part 17'' by Moriyuki Saito, ``Ichinomiya Nooto'', Shibunkaku Publishing, December 2002. As pointed out by , if we follow the Kokufu Festival tradition which states that the origin of 'Zaikan' was the Ichinomiya dispute due to the merger of Sobu and Isonaga, then the Ichinomiya and Ninomiya system existed in Sagami Province in the 7th century, earlier than {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya#Establishment|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|lt=Common theory|1=Ichinomiya}}.In the "Ankamonincho Shimomon" dated 4th year ({{ill|lt=1238|Q5433|qid=Q5433|1=1238}}), there is a mention of ``Ninomiya Kawaso'', indicating that our company's location was {{ill|lt=Manor (Japan)|Q1887569|qid=Q1887569|lt=Manor|1=Shōen}}. “Basic Research on the Uninomiya System of Medieval Countries” , the ``Ankamonin Choshitamon'' written in {{ill|lt=Masayoshi|Q1327489|qid=Q1327489|1=Shōka (era)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1258|Q5506|qid=Q5506|1=1258}}) and the ``{{j alink|Emperor Kameyama|Q360972|Closed Emperor Kameyama}}Insen'' and the descriptions in the ``Kyoto Higashiyama Gobunko Record'' state that while this was a royal manor with strong ties to Kamakura, it can also be seen as {{ill|lt=Kanto Imperial House|Q11655961|qid=Q11655961|1=Kantō goryō}}. In {{ill|lt=Kencho|Q1194270|qid=Q1194270|1=Kenchō}}4 ({{ill|lt=1252|Q5497|qid=Q5497|1=1252}}), {{ill|lt=Imperial Prince Munetaka|Q708897|qid=Q708897|1=Prince Munetaka}} moved to Kamakura. It is said that when he was sent down, he dedicated a sacred horse to the shrine as a ceremony to mark the beginning of the shogun's life, and an article in "Azuma Kagami" dated April 14, 4th year of Kencho states that he dedicated a sacred horse to a major shrine below Tsurugaoka Hachimangu Shrine.According to the Kawago Shrine Journal , the shrine's treasures were destroyed by fire during the {{ill|lt=Oei|Q1193078|qid=Q1193078|lt=Oei era|1=Ōei}} ({{ill|lt=1394|Q6398|qid=Q6398|1=1394}} - {{ill|lt=1427|Q6305|qid=Q6305|1=1427}}), leaving only the wooden structure of the shrine. The "Shinhen Sagami Province Fudoki Koko" states that the ancient legend of the area was lost due to this fire.{{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|qid=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}}4th year ({{ill|lt=1561|Q6618|qid=Q6618|1=1561}}) {{ill|lt=Kenshin Uesugi|Q311080|qid=Q311080|1=Uesugi Kenshin}} {{ill|lt=Oda Hara Castle|Q1013437|qid=Q1013437|1=Odawara Castle}} The shrine building was burnt down by the fire of the expedition, but after that {{ill|lt=Motogame|Q1145064|qid=Q1145064|lt=Motoki year|1=Genki (era)}} ({{ill|lt=1570|Q6659|qid=Q6659|1=1570}} - Rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Gohojo clan|Q431928|qid=Q431928|1=Later Hōjō clan}} in {{ill|lt=1573|Q6678|qid=Q6678|1=1573}}). According to the Kawago Shrine Magazine , our shrine was located in the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ushitora|1=UNKNOWN}} direction of Odawara Castle, and was especially revered by the Gohojo clan as {{ill|lt=Kimon|Q11673539|qid=Q11673539|1=Kimon}} as a guardian deity. According to "Fundamental Research on the Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries" , it is known that during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, ``Futami Minbujo'', who is thought to be a priest, went to war following the Gohojo clan to {{ill|lt=Shimousa Province|Q1053695|qid=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}} Sekijuku, and {{ill|lt=Tenmon (era name)|Q1192853|qid=Q1192853|lt=Tenmon|1=Tenbun}}{{ill|lt=Rokusho Shrine on December 23, 1544|Q6525|qid=Q6525|1=1544}} (Oiso Town)|Q11392352|Q11392352|Rokusho Shrine}} It is said that the Gohojo clan and our company had a close relationship, as the ``Ninomiya Myojin Tango Festival'' can be seen on the ``Hojo family's red seal letter'' addressed to the monks and priests of Rokusho Shrine.{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}19 ({{ill|lt=1591|Q6766|qid=Q6766|1=1591}}) {{ill|lt=Ieyasu Tokugawa|Q171977|qid=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} was {{jalink|Bunroku When he went to Kyushu {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q711685|qid=Q711685|lt=Nagoya|1=Nagoya Castle (Hizen Province)}} for the Keicho War|Q576338|Deployment of troops to Korea}}, he presented a prayer tag to our company and received 50 {{jalink|stones} of the Goshuinji. (unit)|Q855048|stone}} was donated. After that, he was revered by the Tokugawa family, and in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, he would go to {{ill|lt=Edo Castle|Q865913|qid=Q865913|1=Edo Castle}} every year {{ill|lt=New Year|Q701398|qid=Q701398|1=Japanese New Year}} and present {{ill|lt=God talisman|Q2500908|qid=Q2500908|1=Ofuda}}, and this continued until the end of the Edo period. Although the shrine building was severely damaged by a rainstorm in the 9th year of An'ei ({{ill|lt=1780|Q7716|qid=Q7716|1=1780}}, the priest Futami clan rebuilt it in the 7th year of {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|qid=Q1194121|1=Tenmei}} ({{ill|lt=1787|Q7763|qid=Q7763|1=1787}}), and the shrine remained in use until the early {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} period.In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6), it was promoted to Gosha, and in {{ill|lt=1932|Q18743|qid=Q18743|1=1932}} (Showa 7), it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Construction of the current shrine building began after receiving the notification that the shrine would be promoted to a prefectural shrine, but due to the effects of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=World War II|1=UNKNOWN}} and the abolition of {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} after the war, it was completed 19 years later, in {{ill|lt=1951|Q18591|qid=Q18591|1=1951}} (Showa 26). {{ill|lt=2011|Q1994|qid=Q1994|1=2011}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|qid=Q234510|1=Heisei era}}23) {{ill|lt=September 21|Q2891|qid=Q2891|1=September 21}} Typhoon No. 15|Q1136789|Typhoon No. 15}} caused damage, including one of the couple's {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} falling down on the temple grounds and the roof of the {{ill|lt=kagura hall|Q94498|qid=Q94498|1=Kagura-den}} being destroyed. === Precincts === The forest of shrines and temples, mainly consisting of black pine and cedar, is designated as a natural environment conservation area in Kanagawa Prefecture. =Designation status of Kanagawa Prefecture Natural Environment Conservation Area|accessdate=2024-12-05}}. File:Kawawa-shrine 2009-11.jpg|Shrine of worship File:Kawawa jinja Honden (back).JPG|Main shrine File:Kawawa-jinja, Higashi-gosha.jpg|Togosha File:Kawawa-jinja, Nishi-gosha.jpg|Nishigosha File:Kawawa-jinja(Ninomiya) Kaguraden.jpg|Kaguraden File:Kawawa-jinja(Ninomiya) zuishinmon.jpg|Zuishinmon File:Kawawa-jinja, zuishinmon.jpg|Zuishinmon (from another angle) === Festival === * {{ill|lt=New Year's Day|Q196627|qid=Q196627|lt=New Year's Day|1=New Year's Day}} Prayer Festival (New Year's Day) * Otsutsugayu Festival (January 15th) * {{ill|lt=Setsubun|Q746798|qid=Q746798|1=Setsubun}} Festival (February 3rd) * Prayer Festival (February) * {{ill|lt=Kokufu Festival|Q11420919|qid=Q11420919|1=Kokufu-sai}} (May 5th) * Natsugoe {{ill|lt=Great Purification|Q11261910|qid=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}} (late June) * {{ill|lt=Reitaisai|Q11385469|qid=Q11385469|lt=Reitaisai|1=Reisai}} (2nd Sunday of October) * {{ill|lt=Ninamesai|Q11501518|qid=Q11501518|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}} (November 23rd) * {{ill|lt=December|Q126|qid=Q126|lt=December|1=December}} Great purification (late December) === Cultural assets === === Cultural property designated by Ninomiya Town === * Important {{ill|lt=Tangible Cultural Property|Q2901860|qid=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}} ** Two wooden standing statues (sculptures) - Designated in March 2007. ** Tabune (archaeological material) - Estimated to be {{ill|lt=Nara period|Q189178|qid=Q189178|1=Nara period}}. Excavated in {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|qid=Q2084|1=1915}} ({{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|qid=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}4) from a rice field in the former Shinto territory. Designated on June 5, 1974. ** Ancient documents (historical materials) - 11 items including letters and copies of letters of donation. Designated on June 5, 1974. === Local information === ; Location * {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|qid=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nakagun|Q1202849|qid=Q1202849|1=Naka District, Kanagawa}}{{ill|lt=Ninomiya Town|Q1354853|qid=Q1354853|1=Ninomiya, Kanagawa}}Yamanishi 2122 ; Transportation access * {{ill|lt=Tokaido Line (JR East)|Q11176477|qid=Q11176477|lt=JR Tokaido Line|1=Tōkaidō Line (JR East)}} {{ill|lt=Ninomiya Station|Q511262|qid=Q511262|1=Ninomiya Station}} From the north exit, take the {{ill|lt=Ninomiya Town Community Bus|Q11371504|qid=Q11371504|1=Ninomiya Town Community Bus}} "Nino♥ Bus" and get off at the "Kawagami Shrine Iriguchi" bus stop, about a 6-minute walk. *Only operates on weekdays.{{Cite web |title=Sagami Province Ninomiya Kawago Shrine Access |url=https://kawawajinja.com/acs/komyu/index.html |website=kawawajinja.com |access-date=2024-07-29}} * {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway|Q499071|qid=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Tokaido Main Line|Q1190152|qid=Q1190152|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}} from {{ill|lt=Ninomiya Station|Q511262|qid=Q511262|1=Ninomiya Station}} to {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu|Q10854960|qid=Q10854960|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu}} bus {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu Hadano Office #Ninomiya Station - Nakai Town Hall|Q11588881|qid=Q11588881|lt=Route 230|1=Route 230}} Take the train bound for "{{ill|lt=Nakai Town|Q1344396|qid=Q1344396|1=Nakai, Kanagawa}} Hall Entrance" ** Get off at "Oshikiri Sakaue" and walk for about 14 minutes. ** About 11 minutes walk from "Kaneyabu".* About 15 minutes by car from {{ill|lt=Seisho Bypass|Q7446950|qid=Q7446950|1=Seishō Bypass}}{{ill|lt=Nishisho Ninomiya Interchange|Q11629118|qid=Q11629118|1=Seisho-Ninomiya interchange}}. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} ; annotation ; Source {{Reflist}} === References === * Edited by {{ill|lt=Ito Ashida|Q17988362|qid=Q17988362|1=Koreto Ashida}} "{{ill|lt=Dainippon Topographical Series|Q11435652|qid=Q11435652|1=Dai-Nihon Chishi Taikei}}15 {{ill|lt=New Sagami Province Fudoki Kou|Q11503942|qid=Q11503942|1=Shinpen Sagami no Kuni Fudoki-kō}} Volume 2" {{ill|lt=Yuzankaku|Q11659206|qid=Q11659206|1=Yuzankaku}} September 1962 * {{ill|lt=Katsumi Kuroboard|Q3894478|qid=Q3894478|1=Katsumi Kuroita}} Edited by Kokushi Taikei Editorial Committee "{{ill|lt=Kokushi Taikei|Q10926780|qid=Q10926780|lt=Kokushi Taikei|1=Kokushi Taikei}} Volume 32 {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Azuma Kan|1=UNKNOWN}} Part 1" {{ill|lt=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|Q4346755|qid=Q4346755|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan}} July 1964 *Kokushi Taikei Edited by Katsumi Kuroita, Kokushi Taikei Volume 33, Azuma Kango Edition, Yoshikawa Kobunkan, February 1965 * Edited by Katsumi Kuroita, Kokushi Taikei Editorial Committee, “Kokushi Taikei Volume 1, Part 1 {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}” Yoshikawa Kobunkan, December 1966 * National Shrine Directory Publication Society Historiographical Center (ed.) "National Shrine Directory Volume 1" National Shrine Directory Publication Society Historiographical Center July 1977 * Compiled by Shinto Taikei Compilation Association "Shinto Taikei Shrines Edition 16 Suruga, Izu, Kai, Sagami Province" Shinto Taikei Compilation Association March 1980 * Edited by {{ill|lt=Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596|qid=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}} "Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites - 11 Kanto" {{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|qid=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}} December 1984 * Edited by the Medieval Countries Ichinomiya System Study Group, “Basic Research on the Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries” Iwata Shoin, February 2000 * Moriyuki Saito “Ichinomiya Nooto” Shibunkaku Publishing December 2002 === Related items === * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinai companies in Sagami country|Q11582137|qid=Q11582137|1=List of Shikinaisha in Sagami Province}} === External link === {{commonscat|Kawawa-jinja}} * [http://kawawajinja.com/ Kawago Shrine] - Official website * {{Official blog}} * [http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=769&cd=1209212&scd=&npg=0 Kawago Shrine] - Kanagawa Prefectural Shrine Office * {{youTube|p=PLWqzfKsd3jigUmIsKJJsdipyrDuLOzvO-|Story of the Chief Priest of Kawago Shrine}} {{Shinto horizontal}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kawawa Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kawawa Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kanagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:二宮町の建築物]] [[Category:二宮町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sagami Province]] [[Category:神奈川県の旧県社]] [[Category:Sagami Province]] [[Category:Ni-no-Miya]]

{{ill|lt=Sagami country|Q1053732|qid=Q1053732|1=Sagami Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya #Ninomiya, Sannomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}
Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}} |Foundation = (legendary) 11th {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|qid=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}} |Main shrine={{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternative name = Ninomiya Daimyojin/Ninomiya Myojin Shrine |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival=2nd Sunday of October|Shinto ritual = Otsutsugayu Festival ({{ill|lt=January 15th|Q2260|qid=Q2260|1=January 15}})
{{ill|lt=Kokufu Festival|Q11420919|qid=Q11420919|1=Kokufu-sai}} ({{ill|lt=May 5th|Q2550|qid=Q2550|1=May 5}}) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|lat_deg=35|lat_min=18|lat_sec=0.6|lon_deg=139|lon_min=14|lon_sec=17.1|label=Kawagu Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|18|0.6|N|139|14|17.1|E}}}} }} [[File:Kawawa-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]]'''Kawawa Shrine'' is located in {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|qid=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nakagun|Q1202849|qid=Q1202849|1=Naka District, Kanagawa}}{{ill|lt=Ninomiya Town|Q1354853|qid=Q1354853|1=Ninomiya, Kanagawa}} in Yamanishi. {{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} It is written in "{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}" in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}). It is said to be one of the 13 Shikinai shrines in Engi Province (small shrines) in {{ill|lt=Sagami Province|Q1053732|qid=Q1053732|1=Sagami Province}}. {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}}.A traditional {{ill|lt=festival|Q1322|qid=Q1322|lt=41|1=José Manuel Balmaceda}}, {{ill|lt=Kokufu Festival|Q11420919|qid=Q11420919|lt=Kounomachi|1=Kokufu-sai}} is one of the five Sagami shrines that participates in {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya #Ninomiya, Sannomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}. The main ritual of the festival, ``Zaikan'', is a combination of {{ill|lt=Aibu Kunizo|Q11582190|qid=Q11582190|lt=Aibu|1=sagamu no kuni no miyatsuko}} (Sagamu) and {{ill|lt=Shicho Kunizo|Q11481128|qid=Q11481128|lt=Isonaga|1=Isonaga}} (Shinaga, also written as ``Shicho''), which is the name of Sagami Province. This is said to have been caused by {{ill|lt=history|Q1210892|qid=Q1210892|1=Chinese proverbs}}, who fought over whether {{ill|lt=Samukawa Shrine|Q48744|qid=Q48744|1=Samukawa Shrine}} or our company should be designated as Sagami Province {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}}. The name of the location, ``Ninomiya Town,'' is a nod to our company, and has also been referred to as ``''Ninomiya Daimyojin'''' or ``''Ninomiya Myojin Shrine'''' since ancient times. === Enshrined God === ``Kawagata Shrine Magazine''``Kawagata Shrine Magazine'' is a bookmark for worship distributed by our company. According to , the following five deities are currently enshrined. * {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|qid=Q276944|lt=Daimyo Takamikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}} (Onamuji no Mikoto) - The god who cultivated the land of Japan. * {{jalink|Oimonoimino no Mikoto|Q3270544|Oimonoimino Mikoto} - A god who was instrumental in promoting industry. * {{ill|lt=Shinatsuhiko|Q1073925|qid=Q1073925|lt=Class Nagatsuhiko no Mikoto|1=Shinatsuhiko}} (Shinatsuhiko no Mikoto) - God of wind and breath. * Class Nagatsuhime no Mikoto (Shinatsuhime no Mikoto) - Same as above. A couple deity who pioneered the country of Isonaga. * {{ill|lt=Kotori Hime|Q11626419|qid=Q11626419|lt=Kutori Hime Mikoto|1=Princess Sotoorihime}} (Sotohorihime no Mikoto) - A miraculous god who protects safe childbirth."Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Places - 11 Kanto" Kenichi Tanikawa (ed.) From "Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Places - 11 Kanto" Hakusuisha, December 1984. According to , "{{ill|lt=New Edition of Sagami Province Fudoki|Q11503942|qid=Q11503942|1=Shinpen Sagami no Kuni Fudoki-kō}} Volume 40" compiled in {{ill|lt=Tenpo|Q1369045|qid=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}}12 ({{ill|lt=1841|Q7622|qid=Q7622|1=1841}}) Edited by Ito Ashida From "Dainippon Topographical Series 15, New Sagami Province Fudoki Draft Volume 2", Yuzankaku, September 1962. In , there are three deities enshrined: Itsuhime no Mikoto, Omonoki no Mikoto, and Kura Nagatsuhiko no Mikoto, but in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasunaga|1=UNKNOWN}} 5th year ({{ill|lt=1776|Q7703|qid=Q7703|1=1776}}), {{jalink|chief priest|Q1145} 3500}} From the description in the ``Osai-no-Shiki Tradition'', which was transcribed by Mr. Futami, it can be confirmed that {{ill|lt=Hachimanjin|Q261637|qid=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} was enshrined in the middle of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}. Regarding this, "Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites - 11 Kanto" "Ninomiya Kawago Shrine Engi-sho" was compiled by {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|qid=Q15888735|1=Kannushi (disambiguation)}} Shintaro Futami and is owned by Kawago Shrine. It is also included in ``Shinto Taikei Shrines Edition 16 Suruga, Izu, Kai, and Sagami Province'' (March 1980), edited by the Shinto Taikei Compilation Association. According to , during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|qid=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, {{ill|lt=Taaya County|Q17177016|qid=Q17177016|lt=Yoaya|1=Yurugi district}} and {{jalink|Ashigarashimo County|Q1193172|Ashi It is said that when the east and west coasts of both districts were Isonaga Province, Ayaha Miyatsuko, the ruler of Isonaga Province {{ill|lt=kokuzō|Q2483673|qid=Q2483673|lt=kokuji|1=Kuni no miyatsuko}}, founded it to protect the country in accordance with an imperial order. ``Shikinaisha Investigation Report Volume 11'' also states that this area was the center of Isonagakuni, and that our shrine was connected to {{ill|lt=Shinagakunizo|Q11481128|qid=Q11481128|lt=Isonagakunizo|1=Isonagakunizo}} (Shinaga no Kuni no Miyako). According to "Shinhen Sagami Province Fudoki Kou Volume 40" , the place name ``Kawago'' is said to come from the {{ill|lt=Nakamura River (Ashigarakami District)|Q11365555|qid=Q11365555|lt=Oshikiri River|1=Nakamura River}} flowing in this area in ancient times, and the name of Kawago Shrine is also derived from the place name.Furthermore, “Ninomiyagawa Kojinsha Lucky Book”The reason why she is considered the wife of Emperor Konkyo is based on the description in ``{{ill|lt=Nihonshoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}} Volume 13.'' It is said that they prayed for the safe birth of the prince, but ``Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites - 11 Kanto'' says that all of this is nothing more than folklore. In {{ill|lt=Extension (era name)|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Extension|1=Enchō}} in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}), it was elevated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi-sha|1=Shikinaisha}} and small shrine according to the ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}''. It is said that {{ill|lt=MINAMOTO no Yoshiie|Q32613|qid=Q32613|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} made offerings during the {{ill|lt=Early Nine Years' War|Q1187448|qid=Q1187448|1=Former Nine Years' War}} and {{ill|lt=Gōsannen's War|Q1187411|qid=Q1187411|1=Gosannen War}}."{{ill|lt=Azuma Kagami|Q1042324|qid=Q1042324|1=Azuma Kagami}}" {{ill|lt=Takehisa|Q1193933|qid=Q1193933|1=Kenkyū}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1192|Q19739|qid=Q19739|1=1192}}) Article dated August 9th It is said that Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} prayed to ``Ninomiya Kawano Daimyojin'' for the safe delivery of {{ill|lt=Masako Hojo|Q463961|qid=Q463961|1=Hōjō Masako}}, and {{ill|lt=Shinba|Q11591047|qid=Q11591047|1=Shinme}} was dedicated to it. In addition, Minamoto no Yoritomo constructed a shrine building and donated some shrine territory during the Kenkyu era. Our company was called Ninomiya {{ill|lt=Myojin|Q5369590|qid=Q5369590|lt=Daimyojin|1=Myōjin}}, Ninomiya Myojin Shrine, but from ``Basic Research on the Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries'' edited by the Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries Research Group, Iwata Shoin, February 2000. states that this ``Azuma Kagami'' is the first document that refers to our company as Ninomiya. However, from ``Ichinomiya Nooto Nooto Part 17'' by Moriyuki Saito, ``Ichinomiya Nooto'', Shibunkaku Publishing, December 2002. As pointed out by , if we follow the Kokufu Festival tradition which states that the origin of 'Zaikan' was the Ichinomiya dispute due to the merger of Sobu and Isonaga, then the Ichinomiya and Ninomiya system existed in Sagami Province in the 7th century, earlier than {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya#Establishment|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|lt=Common theory|1=Ichinomiya}}.In the "Ankamonincho Shimomon" dated 4th year ({{ill|lt=1238|Q5433|qid=Q5433|1=1238}}), there is a mention of ``Ninomiya Kawaso'', indicating that our company's location was {{ill|lt=Manor (Japan)|Q1887569|qid=Q1887569|lt=Manor|1=Shōen}}. “Basic Research on the Uninomiya System of Medieval Countries” , the ``Ankamonin Choshitamon'' written in {{ill|lt=Masayoshi|Q1327489|qid=Q1327489|1=Shōka (era)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1258|Q5506|qid=Q5506|1=1258}}) and the ``{{j alink|Emperor Kameyama|Q360972|Closed Emperor Kameyama}}Insen'' and the descriptions in the ``Kyoto Higashiyama Gobunko Record'' state that while this was a royal manor with strong ties to Kamakura, it can also be seen as {{ill|lt=Kanto Imperial House|Q11655961|qid=Q11655961|1=Kantō goryō}}. In {{ill|lt=Kencho|Q1194270|qid=Q1194270|1=Kenchō}}4 ({{ill|lt=1252|Q5497|qid=Q5497|1=1252}}), {{ill|lt=Imperial Prince Munetaka|Q708897|qid=Q708897|1=Prince Munetaka}} moved to Kamakura. It is said that when he was sent down, he dedicated a sacred horse to the shrine as a ceremony to mark the beginning of the shogun's life, and an article in "Azuma Kagami" dated April 14, 4th year of Kencho states that he dedicated a sacred horse to a major shrine below Tsurugaoka Hachimangu Shrine.According to the Kawago Shrine Journal , the shrine's treasures were destroyed by fire during the {{ill|lt=Oei|Q1193078|qid=Q1193078|lt=Oei era|1=Ōei}} ({{ill|lt=1394|Q6398|qid=Q6398|1=1394}} - {{ill|lt=1427|Q6305|qid=Q6305|1=1427}}), leaving only the wooden structure of the shrine. The "Shinhen Sagami Province Fudoki Koko" states that the ancient legend of the area was lost due to this fire.{{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|qid=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}}4th year ({{ill|lt=1561|Q6618|qid=Q6618|1=1561}}) {{ill|lt=Kenshin Uesugi|Q311080|qid=Q311080|1=Uesugi Kenshin}} {{ill|lt=Oda Hara Castle|Q1013437|qid=Q1013437|1=Odawara Castle}} The shrine building was burnt down by the fire of the expedition, but after that {{ill|lt=Motogame|Q1145064|qid=Q1145064|lt=Motoki year|1=Genki (era)}} ({{ill|lt=1570|Q6659|qid=Q6659|1=1570}} - Rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Gohojo clan|Q431928|qid=Q431928|1=Later Hōjō clan}} in {{ill|lt=1573|Q6678|qid=Q6678|1=1573}}). According to the Kawago Shrine Magazine , our shrine was located in the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ushitora|1=UNKNOWN}} direction of Odawara Castle, and was especially revered by the Gohojo clan as {{ill|lt=Kimon|Q11673539|qid=Q11673539|1=Kimon}} as a guardian deity. According to "Fundamental Research on the Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries" , it is known that during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, ``Futami Minbujo'', who is thought to be a priest, went to war following the Gohojo clan to {{ill|lt=Shimousa Province|Q1053695|qid=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}} Sekijuku, and {{ill|lt=Tenmon (era name)|Q1192853|qid=Q1192853|lt=Tenmon|1=Tenbun}}{{ill|lt=Rokusho Shrine on December 23, 1544|Q6525|qid=Q6525|1=1544}} (Oiso Town)|Q11392352|Q11392352|Rokusho Shrine}} It is said that the Gohojo clan and our company had a close relationship, as the ``Ninomiya Myojin Tango Festival'' can be seen on the ``Hojo family's red seal letter'' addressed to the monks and priests of Rokusho Shrine.{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}19 ({{ill|lt=1591|Q6766|qid=Q6766|1=1591}}) {{ill|lt=Ieyasu Tokugawa|Q171977|qid=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} was {{jalink|Bunroku When he went to Kyushu {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q711685|qid=Q711685|lt=Nagoya|1=Nagoya Castle (Hizen Province)}} for the Keicho War|Q576338|Deployment of troops to Korea}}, he presented a prayer tag to our company and received 50 {{jalink|stones} of the Goshuinji. (unit)|Q855048|stone}} was donated. After that, he was revered by the Tokugawa family, and in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, he would go to {{ill|lt=Edo Castle|Q865913|qid=Q865913|1=Edo Castle}} every year {{ill|lt=New Year|Q701398|qid=Q701398|1=Japanese New Year}} and present {{ill|lt=God talisman|Q2500908|qid=Q2500908|1=Ofuda}}, and this continued until the end of the Edo period. Although the shrine building was severely damaged by a rainstorm in the 9th year of An'ei ({{ill|lt=1780|Q7716|qid=Q7716|1=1780}}, the priest Futami clan rebuilt it in the 7th year of {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|qid=Q1194121|1=Tenmei}} ({{ill|lt=1787|Q7763|qid=Q7763|1=1787}}), and the shrine remained in use until the early {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} period.In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6), it was promoted to Gosha, and in {{ill|lt=1932|Q18743|qid=Q18743|1=1932}} (Showa 7), it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Construction of the current shrine building began after receiving the notification that the shrine would be promoted to a prefectural shrine, but due to the effects of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=World War II|1=UNKNOWN}} and the abolition of {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} after the war, it was completed 19 years later, in {{ill|lt=1951|Q18591|qid=Q18591|1=1951}} (Showa 26). {{ill|lt=2011|Q1994|qid=Q1994|1=2011}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|qid=Q234510|1=Heisei era}}23) {{ill|lt=September 21|Q2891|qid=Q2891|1=September 21}} Typhoon No. 15|Q1136789|Typhoon No. 15}} caused damage, including one of the couple's {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} falling down on the temple grounds and the roof of the {{ill|lt=kagura hall|Q94498|qid=Q94498|1=Kagura-den}} being destroyed. === Precincts === The forest of shrines and temples, mainly consisting of black pine and cedar, is designated as a natural environment conservation area in Kanagawa Prefecture. =Designation status of Kanagawa Prefecture Natural Environment Conservation Area|accessdate=2024-12-05}}. Excavated in {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|qid=Q2084|1=1915}} ({{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|qid=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}4) from a rice field in the former Shinto territory. {{ill|lt=Kokufu Festival|Q11420919|qid=Q11420919|1=Kokufu-sai}} ({{ill|lt=May 5th|Q2550|qid=Q2550|1=May 5}}) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|lat_deg=35|lat_min=18|lat_sec=0.6|lon_deg=139|lon_min=14|lon_sec=17.1|label=Kawagu Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|18|0.6|N|139|14|17.1|E}}}} }} [[File:Kawawa-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]]'''Kawawa Shrine'' is located in {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|qid=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nakagun|Q1202849|qid=Q1202849|1=Naka District, Kanagawa}}{{ill|lt=Ninomiya Town|Q1354853|qid=Q1354853|1=Ninomiya, Kanagawa}} in Yamanishi. {{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} It is written in "{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}" in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}). It is said to be one of the 13 Shikinai shrines in Engi Province (small shrines) in {{ill|lt=Sagami Province|Q1053732|qid=Q1053732|1=Sagami Province}}. {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}}.A traditional {{ill|lt=festival|Q1322|qid=Q1322|lt=41|1=José Manuel Balmaceda}}, {{ill|lt=Kokufu Festival|Q11420919|qid=Q11420919|lt=Kounomachi|1=Kokufu-sai}} is one of the five Sagami shrines that participates in {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya #Ninomiya, Sannomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}. The main ritual of the festival, ``Zaikan'', is a combination of {{ill|lt=Aibu Kunizo|Q11582190|qid=Q11582190|lt=Aibu|1=sagamu no kuni no miyatsuko}} (Sagamu) and {{ill|lt=Shicho Kunizo|Q11481128|qid=Q11481128|lt=Isonaga|1=Isonaga}} (Shinaga, also written as ``Shicho''), which is the name of Sagami Province. This is said to have been caused by {{ill|lt=history|Q1210892|qid=Q1210892|1=Chinese proverbs}}, who fought over whether {{ill|lt=Samukawa Shrine|Q48744|qid=Q48744|1=Samukawa Shrine}} or our company should be designated as Sagami Province {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}}. The name of the location, ``Ninomiya Town,'' is a nod to our company, and has also been referred to as ``''Ninomiya Daimyojin'''' or ``''Ninomiya Myojin Shrine'''' since ancient times. === Enshrined God === ``Kawagata Shrine Magazine''``Kawagata Shrine Magazine'' is a bookmark for worship distributed by our company. According to , the following five deities are currently enshrined. * {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|qid=Q276944|lt=Daimyo Takamikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}} (Onamuji no Mikoto) - The god who cultivated the land of Japan. * {{jalink|Oimonoimino no Mikoto|Q3270544|Oimonoimino Mikoto} - A god who was instrumental in promoting industry. * {{ill|lt=Shinatsuhiko|Q1073925|qid=Q1073925|lt=Class Nagatsuhiko no Mikoto|1=Shinatsuhiko}} (Shinatsuhiko no Mikoto) - God of wind and breath. * Class Nagatsuhime no Mikoto (Shinatsuhime no Mikoto) - Same as above. A couple deity who pioneered the country of Isonaga. * {{ill|lt=Kotori Hime|Q11626419|qid=Q11626419|lt=Kutori Hime Mikoto|1=Princess Sotoorihime}} (Sotohorihime no Mikoto) - A miraculous god who protects safe childbirth."Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Places - 11 Kanto" Kenichi Tanikawa (ed.) From "Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Places - 11 Kanto" Hakusuisha, December 1984. According to , "{{ill|lt=New Edition of Sagami Province Fudoki|Q11503942|qid=Q11503942|1=Shinpen Sagami no Kuni Fudoki-kō}} Volume 40" compiled in {{ill|lt=Tenpo|Q1369045|qid=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}}12 ({{ill|lt=1841|Q7622|qid=Q7622|1=1841}}) Edited by Ito Ashida From "Dainippon Topographical Series 15, New Sagami Province Fudoki Draft Volume 2", Yuzankaku, September 1962. In , there are three deities enshrined: Itsuhime no Mikoto, Omonoki no Mikoto, and Kura Nagatsuhiko no Mikoto, but in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasunaga|1=UNKNOWN}} 5th year ({{ill|lt=1776|Q7703|qid=Q7703|1=1776}}), {{jalink|chief priest|Q1145} 3500}} From the description in the ``Osai-no-Shiki Tradition'', which was transcribed by Mr. Futami, it can be confirmed that {{ill|lt=Hachimanjin|Q261637|qid=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} was enshrined in the middle of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}. Regarding this, "Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites - 11 Kanto" "Ninomiya Kawago Shrine Engi-sho" was compiled by {{ill|lt=Shinto priest|Q15888735|qid=Q15888735|1=Kannushi (disambiguation)}} Shintaro Futami and is owned by Kawago Shrine. It is also included in ``Shinto Taikei Shrines Edition 16 Suruga, Izu, Kai, and Sagami Province'' (March 1980), edited by the Shinto Taikei Compilation Association. According to , during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|qid=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, {{ill|lt=Taaya County|Q17177016|qid=Q17177016|lt=Yoaya|1=Yurugi district}} and {{jalink|Ashigarashimo County|Q1193172|Ashi It is said that when the east and west coasts of both districts were Isonaga Province, Ayaha Miyatsuko, the ruler of Isonaga Province {{ill|lt=kokuzō|Q2483673|qid=Q2483673|lt=kokuji|1=Kuni no miyatsuko}}, founded it to protect the country in accordance with an imperial order. ``Shikinaisha Investigation Report Volume 11'' also states that this area was the center of Isonagakuni, and that our shrine was connected to {{ill|lt=Shinagakunizo|Q11481128|qid=Q11481128|lt=Isonagakunizo|1=Isonagakunizo}} (Shinaga no Kuni no Miyako). According to "Shinhen Sagami Province Fudoki Kou Volume 40" , the place name ``Kawago'' is said to come from the {{ill|lt=Nakamura River (Ashigarakami District)|Q11365555|qid=Q11365555|lt=Oshikiri River|1=Nakamura River}} flowing in this area in ancient times, and the name of Kawago Shrine is also derived from the place name.Furthermore, “Ninomiyagawa Kojinsha Lucky Book”The reason why she is considered the wife of Emperor Konkyo is based on the description in ``{{ill|lt=Nihonshoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}} Volume 13.'' It is said that they prayed for the safe birth of the prince, but ``Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites - 11 Kanto'' says that all of this is nothing more than folklore. In {{ill|lt=Extension (era name)|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Extension|1=Enchō}} in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}), it was elevated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi-sha|1=Shikinaisha}} and small shrine according to the ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}''. It is said that {{ill|lt=MINAMOTO no Yoshiie|Q32613|qid=Q32613|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} made offerings during the {{ill|lt=Early Nine Years' War|Q1187448|qid=Q1187448|1=Former Nine Years' War}} and {{ill|lt=Gōsannen's War|Q1187411|qid=Q1187411|1=Gosannen War}}."{{ill|lt=Azuma Kagami|Q1042324|qid=Q1042324|1=Azuma Kagami}}" {{ill|lt=Takehisa|Q1193933|qid=Q1193933|1=Kenkyū}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1192|Q19739|qid=Q19739|1=1192}}) Article dated August 9th It is said that Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} prayed to ``Ninomiya Kawano Daimyojin'' for the safe delivery of {{ill|lt=Masako Hojo|Q463961|qid=Q463961|1=Hōjō Masako}}, and {{ill|lt=Shinba|Q11591047|qid=Q11591047|1=Shinme}} was dedicated to it. In addition, Minamoto no Yoritomo constructed a shrine building and donated some shrine territory during the Kenkyu era. Our company was called Ninomiya {{ill|lt=Myojin|Q5369590|qid=Q5369590|lt=Daimyojin|1=Myōjin}}, Ninomiya Myojin Shrine, but from ``Basic Research on the Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries'' edited by the Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries Research Group, Iwata Shoin, February 2000. states that this ``Azuma Kagami'' is the first document that refers to our company as Ninomiya. However, from ``Ichinomiya Nooto Nooto Part 17'' by Moriyuki Saito, ``Ichinomiya Nooto'', Shibunkaku Publishing, December 2002. As pointed out by , if we follow the Kokufu Festival tradition which states that the origin of 'Zaikan' was the Ichinomiya dispute due to the merger of Sobu and Isonaga, then the Ichinomiya and Ninomiya system existed in Sagami Province in the 7th century, earlier than {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya#Establishment|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|lt=Common theory|1=Ichinomiya}}.In the "Ankamonincho Shimomon" dated 4th year ({{ill|lt=1238|Q5433|qid=Q5433|1=1238}}), there is a mention of ``Ninomiya Kawaso'', indicating that our company's location was {{ill|lt=Manor (Japan)|Q1887569|qid=Q1887569|lt=Manor|1=Shōen}}. “Basic Research on the Uninomiya System of Medieval Countries” , the ``Ankamonin Choshitamon'' written in {{ill|lt=Masayoshi|Q1327489|qid=Q1327489|1=Shōka (era)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1258|Q5506|qid=Q5506|1=1258}}) and the ``{{j alink|Emperor Kameyama|Q360972|Closed Emperor Kameyama}}Insen'' and the descriptions in the ``Kyoto Higashiyama Gobunko Record'' state that while this was a royal manor with strong ties to Kamakura, it can also be seen as {{ill|lt=Kanto Imperial House|Q11655961|qid=Q11655961|1=Kantō goryō}}. In {{ill|lt=Kencho|Q1194270|qid=Q1194270|1=Kenchō}}4 ({{ill|lt=1252|Q5497|qid=Q5497|1=1252}}), {{ill|lt=Imperial Prince Munetaka|Q708897|qid=Q708897|1=Prince Munetaka}} moved to Kamakura. It is said that when he was sent down, he dedicated a sacred horse to the shrine as a ceremony to mark the beginning of the shogun's life, and an article in "Azuma Kagami" dated April 14, 4th year of Kencho states that he dedicated a sacred horse to a major shrine below Tsurugaoka Hachimangu Shrine.According to the Kawago Shrine Journal , the shrine's treasures were destroyed by fire during the {{ill|lt=Oei|Q1193078|qid=Q1193078|lt=Oei era|1=Ōei}} ({{ill|lt=1394|Q6398|qid=Q6398|1=1394}} - {{ill|lt=1427|Q6305|qid=Q6305|1=1427}}), leaving only the wooden structure of the shrine. The "Shinhen Sagami Province Fudoki Koko" states that the ancient legend of the area was lost due to this fire.{{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|qid=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}}4th year ({{ill|lt=1561|Q6618|qid=Q6618|1=1561}}) {{ill|lt=Kenshin Uesugi|Q311080|qid=Q311080|1=Uesugi Kenshin}} {{ill|lt=Oda Hara Castle|Q1013437|qid=Q1013437|1=Odawara Castle}} The shrine building was burnt down by the fire of the expedition, but after that {{ill|lt=Motogame|Q1145064|qid=Q1145064|lt=Motoki year|1=Genki (era)}} ({{ill|lt=1570|Q6659|qid=Q6659|1=1570}} - Rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Gohojo clan|Q431928|qid=Q431928|1=Later Hōjō clan}} in {{ill|lt=1573|Q6678|qid=Q6678|1=1573}}). According to the Kawago Shrine Magazine , our shrine was located in the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ushitora|1=UNKNOWN}} direction of Odawara Castle, and was especially revered by the Gohojo clan as {{ill|lt=Kimon|Q11673539|qid=Q11673539|1=Kimon}} as a guardian deity. According to "Fundamental Research on the Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries" , it is known that during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, ``Futami Minbujo'', who is thought to be a priest, went to war following the Gohojo clan to {{ill|lt=Shimousa Province|Q1053695|qid=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}} Sekijuku, and {{ill|lt=Tenmon (era name)|Q1192853|qid=Q1192853|lt=Tenmon|1=Tenbun}}{{ill|lt=Rokusho Shrine on December 23, 1544|Q6525|qid=Q6525|1=1544}} (Oiso Town)|Q11392352|Q11392352|Rokusho Shrine}} It is said that the Gohojo clan and our company had a close relationship, as the ``Ninomiya Myojin Tango Festival'' can be seen on the ``Hojo family's red seal letter'' addressed to the monks and priests of Rokusho Shrine.{{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}19 ({{ill|lt=1591|Q6766|qid=Q6766|1=1591}}) {{ill|lt=Ieyasu Tokugawa|Q171977|qid=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} was {{jalink|Bunroku When he went to Kyushu {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle|Q711685|qid=Q711685|lt=Nagoya|1=Nagoya Castle (Hizen Province)}} for the Keicho War|Q576338|Deployment of troops to Korea}}, he presented a prayer tag to our company and received 50 {{jalink|stones} of the Goshuinji. (unit)|Q855048|stone}} was donated. After that, he was revered by the Tokugawa family, and in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, he would go to {{ill|lt=Edo Castle|Q865913|qid=Q865913|1=Edo Castle}} every year {{ill|lt=New Year|Q701398|qid=Q701398|1=Japanese New Year}} and present {{ill|lt=God talisman|Q2500908|qid=Q2500908|1=Ofuda}}, and this continued until the end of the Edo period. Although the shrine building was severely damaged by a rainstorm in the 9th year of An'ei ({{ill|lt=1780|Q7716|qid=Q7716|1=1780}}, the priest Futami clan rebuilt it in the 7th year of {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|qid=Q1194121|1=Tenmei}} ({{ill|lt=1787|Q7763|qid=Q7763|1=1787}}), and the shrine remained in use until the early {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} period.In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6), it was promoted to Gosha, and in {{ill|lt=1932|Q18743|qid=Q18743|1=1932}} (Showa 7), it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. Construction of the current shrine building began after receiving the notification that the shrine would be promoted to a prefectural shrine, but due to the effects of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=World War II|1=UNKNOWN}} and the abolition of {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} after the war, it was completed 19 years later, in {{ill|lt=1951|Q18591|qid=Q18591|1=1951}} (Showa 26). {{ill|lt=2011|Q1994|qid=Q1994|1=2011}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|qid=Q234510|1=Heisei era}}23) {{ill|lt=September 21|Q2891|qid=Q2891|1=September 21}} Typhoon No. 15|Q1136789|Typhoon No. 15}} caused damage, including one of the couple's {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cedar|1=UNKNOWN}} falling down on the temple grounds and the roof of the {{ill|lt=kagura hall|Q94498|qid=Q94498|1=Kagura-den}} being destroyed. === Precincts === The forest of shrines and temples, mainly consisting of black pine and cedar, is designated as a natural environment conservation area in Kanagawa Prefecture. =Designation status of Kanagawa Prefecture Natural Environment Conservation Area|accessdate=2024-12-05}}. File:Kawawa-shrine 2009-11.jpg|Shrine of worship File:Kawawa jinja Honden (back).JPG|Main shrine File:Kawawa-jinja, Higashi-gosha.jpg|Togosha File:Kawawa-jinja, Nishi-gosha.jpg|Nishigosha File:Kawawa-jinja(Ninomiya) Kaguraden.jpg|Kaguraden File:Kawawa-jinja(Ninomiya) zuishinmon.jpg|Zuishinmon File:Kawawa-jinja, zuishinmon.jpg|Zuishinmon (from another angle) === Festival === * {{ill|lt=New Year's Day|Q196627|qid=Q196627|lt=New Year's Day|1=New Year's Day}} Prayer Festival (New Year's Day) * Otsutsugayu Festival (January 15th) * {{ill|lt=Setsubun|Q746798|qid=Q746798|1=Setsubun}} Festival (February 3rd) * Prayer Festival (February) * {{ill|lt=Kokufu Festival|Q11420919|qid=Q11420919|1=Kokufu-sai}} (May 5th) * Natsugoe {{ill|lt=Great Purification|Q11261910|qid=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}} (late June) * {{ill|lt=Reitaisai|Q11385469|qid=Q11385469|lt=Reitaisai|1=Reisai}} (2nd Sunday of October) * {{ill|lt=Ninamesai|Q11501518|qid=Q11501518|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}} (November 23rd) * {{ill|lt=December|Q126|qid=Q126|lt=December|1=December}} Great purification (late December) === Cultural assets === === Cultural property designated by Ninomiya Town === * Important {{ill|lt=Tangible Cultural Property|Q2901860|qid=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}} ** Two wooden standing statues (sculptures) - Designated in March 2007. ** Tabune (archaeological material) - Estimated to be {{ill|lt=Nara period|Q189178|qid=Q189178|1=Nara period}}. Excavated in {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|qid=Q2084|1=1915}} ({{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|qid=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}4) from a rice field in the former Shinto territory. Designated on June 5, 1974. ** Ancient documents (historical materials) - 11 items including letters and copies of letters of donation. Designated on June 5, 1974. === Local information === ; Location * {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|qid=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nakagun|Q1202849|qid=Q1202849|1=Naka District, Kanagawa}}{{ill|lt=Ninomiya Town|Q1354853|qid=Q1354853|1=Ninomiya, Kanagawa}}Yamanishi 2122 ; Transportation access * {{ill|lt=Tokaido Line (JR East)|Q11176477|qid=Q11176477|lt=JR Tokaido Line|1=Tōkaidō Line (JR East)}} {{ill|lt=Ninomiya Station|Q511262|qid=Q511262|1=Ninomiya Station}} From the north exit, take the {{ill|lt=Ninomiya Town Community Bus|Q11371504|qid=Q11371504|1=Ninomiya Town Community Bus}} "Nino♥ Bus" and get off at the "Kawagami Shrine Iriguchi" bus stop, about a 6-minute walk. *Only operates on weekdays.{{Cite web |title=Sagami Province Ninomiya Kawago Shrine Access |url=https://kawawajinja.com/acs/komyu/index.html |website=kawawajinja.com |access-date=2024-07-29}} * {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway|Q499071|qid=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Tokaido Main Line|Q1190152|qid=Q1190152|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}} from {{ill|lt=Ninomiya Station|Q511262|qid=Q511262|1=Ninomiya Station}} to {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu|Q10854960|qid=Q10854960|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu}} bus {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu Hadano Office #Ninomiya Station - Nakai Town Hall|Q11588881|qid=Q11588881|lt=Route 230|1=Route 230}} Take the train bound for "{{ill|lt=Nakai Town|Q1344396|qid=Q1344396|1=Nakai, Kanagawa}} Hall Entrance" ** Get off at "Oshikiri Sakaue" and walk for about 14 minutes. ** About 11 minutes walk from "Kaneyabu".* About 15 minutes by car from {{ill|lt=Seisho Bypass|Q7446950|qid=Q7446950|1=Seishō Bypass}}{{ill|lt=Nishisho Ninomiya Interchange|Q11629118|qid=Q11629118|1=Seisho-Ninomiya interchange}}. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} ;
